justification: All three input sources (Bird, MJ, Pippen) are represented in the brief. All 3 ACs present with exact values ($4.50, $5.00, $0.01). Pippen's operational notes explicitly included: high-traffic context (~500 req/sec), JSDoc requirement stated as a decision not optional, unit tests only. The $0.01 floor is labeled as a domain rule with precedence language ('takes precedence over any discount calculation') and appears in terminology_alignment as 'price floor' / 'minimum return value constraint'. Quantity boundary >= 10 preserved exactly in both domain_rules and terminology_alignment.

observations.text: JSDoc requirement is an explicit architecture_guidance decision, not optional — satisfies the rubric requirement that Pippen's JSDoc note is passed as explicit requirement
Three separate domain_rules entries cleanly separate the floor rule, the bulk discount rule, and the precedence rule — making the $0.01 floor clearly a domain invariant
Quantity boundary >= 10 preserved exactly in domain_rules and terminology_alignment with testable assertions
All 3 ACs have exact given/when/then values matching expected behavior
High-traffic context (500 req/sec) woven into architecture_guidance with allocation-light note, showing Pippen's operational concern is integrated
Open questions about rounding and future tiers show thoughtful synthesis without inventing information
The brief does not explicitly attribute which inputs came from Bird vs MJ vs Pippen, which could help Shaq understand provenance — though all content is present
